■ Energy Protection ActT03

BUDGET 1WEALTH 0STABILITY 2Danger
NarratorAs the morning sun streamed through the window of the Presidential Office, an imminent meeting loomed on my agenda. In a quarter of an hour, I was set to discuss the Energy Protection Act of 1932 with Ministers Graf, Holl, and Wisci.
NarratorThe recent abrogation in the Grand National Assembly, coupled with ongoing dialogues with Gasom, had only served to amplify the discourse surrounding the legislation.
NarratorI was sipping my morning coffee and enjoying a quiet moment of solitude when Deivid arrived, characteristically early.
Deivid Wisci"A very good morning to you, Mr. President. I trust I'm not imposing by arriving ahead of schedule?"
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:48:34 p16245 / c365
  • ▶"Not at all, Deivid. On the contrary, we've now got an extra few minutes to strategize before the meeting."
  • ·"Given the serious topic we're addressing, an early start might be just what we need."
  • ·Gesture towards the seat.
Having extra time to strategize before the meeting will allow for more thorough preparation on this critical topic.
DeepSeek-R1"Not at all, Deivid. On the contrary, we've now got an extra few minutes to strategize before the meeting."
Deivid Wisci"I'm relieved to hear that, Anton."
Deivid Wisci"I depart earlier for my appointments nowadays, as the stairs of the capital have become more of a challenge than I would like to admit."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:49:09 p16371 / c342
  • ·“It's nothing to be ashamed of, Deivid. Would you like some coffee or tea?”
  • ▶"Well, Deivid, it's not the stairs we're battling, but the relentless march of time."
  • ·“The Maroon District and the ministerial buildings are dispersed in a very artificial way. No wonder it takes longer.”
  • ·"I'd like to clear my mind before the ministers arrive."
A light-hearted acknowledgment of age or physical challenges, showing empathy.
DeepSeek-R1"Well, Deivid, it's not the stairs we're battling, but the relentless march of time."
Deivid Wisci"Reflecting on the years I've spent within these walls is a journey in itself. From the time my father first brought me here during the reign of the King, right up to the present."
Deivid Wisci"I often ponder what will endure of our actions and identities. Do they hold any significance in the grand scheme of things?"
NarratorHis gaze drifted, locking onto something beyond the window.
NarratorA colossal zeppelin traversed the sky above the Maroon District. For a fleeting moment, it blotted out the sun before gently veering to the left, letting light flood the office once more.
Deivid Wisci"Ah. I still remember the first airships in Sordland. We purchased them from Qinal after the war, during the birth of what Soll called the 'Second Republic'."
Deivid Wisci"Our infrastructure lay in ruins, and those zeppelins offered a much-needed lifeline, a chance to traverse and rebuild. And then, thanks to our research council's diligent efforts, we started manufacturing our own in Gelsord."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:49:42 p16601 / c275
  • ·"It's fascinating to think how much we depended on foreign help after the war. We've come a long way since then."
  • ▶"The manufacturing of zeppelins in Gelsord was truly a turning point."
  • ·"Do you think we might have leaned too heavily on zeppelins for our transportation needs? Perhaps we should have invested more in rebuilding our roads and railways."
Highlighting the success of local manufacturing aligns with the administration's focus on self-reliance and technological advancement.
DeepSeek-R1"The manufacturing of zeppelins in Gelsord was truly a turning point."
Deivid Wisci"You're quite right. It symbolized our resilience, our determination to regain our footing. It was a beacon of hope, signaling the revival of our battered industry."
Deivid Wisci"Our reliance on others was temporary, a stepping stone towards the self-sufficiency we achieved with the National Autarky Plan. Even though it had downsides, I still consider it a success."
NarratorA pause fell over the room. Deivid's gaze landed on the portrait of Tarquin Soll hanging in the Presidential Office.
NarratorHis eyes lingered on the stern face of the old leader. A sigh escaped his lips.
Deivid Wisci"Now, when I think of the EPA, my mind is carried back to the formative years of post-civil war Holsord in the 1930s. The debates, the passion, the potential for transformation—it was a time of renewed optimism."
Deivid Wisci"It is our duty now to learn from the past and strive for a better future. What applied back then won't apply to now."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:50:17 p16789 / c310
  • ▶"I can see why the Energy Protection Act was so crucial at the time. We had to safeguard our energy sector from foreign interference, especially in the context of the post-civil-war economic situation."
  • ·"Life in Holsord back then was tough. Electricity blackouts, ruined infrastructure, unemployment, gangs..."
  • ·"I remember that Soll's pardons to armed rebels did a lot to quell the rampant unrest."
Option 0 directly addresses the Energy Protection Act's purpose and ties into Deivid's discussion of economic resilience.
DeepSeek-R1"I can see why the Energy Protection Act was so crucial at the time. We had to safeguard our energy sector from foreign interference, especially in the context of the post-civil-war economic situation."
Deivid Wisci"Yes, the EPA helped ensure that the sector remained under our control, limiting the extent of ownership of foreign powers such as Rumburg or Lespia."
Deivid Wisci"Despite the controversies, it was crucial to our recovery and has helped us maintain our energy independence to this day."
Deivid Wisci"I did not agree with all of the measures enacted by the technocratic government of that era. But some were undeniably effective."
Deivid Wisci"In the end, it took a collective effort to reassemble our nation's fragmented pieces. I'm sure you remember those dark days yourself."
Deivid Wisci"I know you were just a student at the Deyr University of Culture. Witnessing what true oppression was like."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:50:53 p16829 / c345

DeepSeek-R1"Those experiences have shaped me, and they continue to inform my decisions as president."
Deivid Wisci"The minorities of this nation have suffered particularly."
NarratorDeivid turned towards the large, framed portrait of his father, Artor Sanaco Wisci, hanging on the wall. Painted with rich, warm hues, it captured the first President of Sordland in his prime.
NarratorA spark of determination gleamed in his eyes, and his mouth was set in a gentle, firm smile - a reflection of his belief in a better future for our country.
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:51:29 p16795 / c342
  • ▶"Your father's legacy still looms large. Has it influenced your own views?"
  • ·"Sordland wouldn't be what it is today without your father."
  • ·"Artor S. Wisci. It's a shame that such an influential man died under such tragic circumstances."
Acknowledging Deivid's reflection on his father's legacy invites deeper engagement.
DeepSeek-R1"Your father's legacy still looms large. Has it influenced your own views?"
Deivid Wisci"My father was a visionary, a man who sought to modernize Sordland into a secular, democratic republic. I've always been guided by his principles."
Deivid Wisci"His ability to mobilize public support for revolutionary change has been a constant inspiration to me."
NarratorHe looked back at the portrait with a mix of pride and sadness.
Deivid Wisci"In a way, Tarquin Soll did carry on my father's legacy. Under his leadership, we transitioned from a military government to a civilian one, and experienced our first multi-party elections."
Deivid Wisci"But over time, he drifted away from the ideals of reform and progress that my father held so dear."
Deivid Wisci"His visionary zeal succumbed to the more pragmatic, more conservative, and sometimes even regressive, dictates of political survival."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:52:00 p16795 / c221
  • ·"Perhaps history would have taken a different course if the Izzam Incident hadn't happened."
  • ▶"Still, one must credit Tarquin Soll for extricating our nation from the ashes."
  • ·"In retrospect, an earlier resignation might have been beneficial. His ingrained traditions seem to have done more harm than good in the long run."
  • ·Keep listening.
Acknowledging Soll's role in rebuilding the nation while hinting at the need for reform.
DeepSeek-R1"Still, one must credit Tarquin Soll for extricating our nation from the ashes."
Deivid Wisci"No one can deny Soll's contribution. His leadership was instrumental in rebuilding Sordland."
Deivid Wisci"Alphonso, for all his flaws, did seek to set the nation back on a path of progress with his focus on a free market and economic liberalization."
Deivid Wisci"His EPA amendments, for example, were a severe departure from Soll's National Autarky Plan. But many praised the way that it opened the doors for unprecedented foreign investment in our energy economy."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:52:34 p16915 / c257
  • ·"The NAP was a cornerstone for our post-war recovery. I wonder, could we have done more with it?"
  • ▶"First it was the State Educators Corps, then the SSC Bills, and then slowly the EPA. He wanted to erase every trace of Tarquin Soll's Second Republic."
  • ·"Those same amendments also opened the door to corruption."
  • ·"Do you think the EPA still holds relevance today, or should we consider a complete overhaul?"

It highlights the pattern of amendments that reflect a departure from Soll's legacy.
DeepSeek-R1"First it was the State Educators Corps, then the SSC Bills, and then slowly the EPA. He wanted to erase every trace of Tarquin Soll's Second Republic."
Deivid Wisci"I believe much of his urgency was motivated by his own desire to escape Soll's shadow. For better or worse, he managed it."
NarratorJust as the conversation hit a pause, the door swung open and the other ministers arrived. Symon Holl entered first with a confident stride.
Symon Holl"Good morning, Mr. President. Deivid. I trust the discussion is progressing well?"
NarratorLileas followed closely behind. She walked in with an air of calm authority, her gaze sweeping across the room.
Lileas Graf"Gentlemen. I hope I'm not interrupting anything crucial."
Deivid Wisci"Welcome, everyone. It's great to see you all here. We have some important discussions on the agenda today, particularly concerning the Energy Protection Act."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:53:10 p16948 / c347
  • ·"Thank you, Deivid. I appreciate the urgency. Let's get right to the heart of the matter."
  • ▶"Indeed, it's a critical issue that we need to address. I look forward to hearing everyone's perspectives."
  • ·"Proceed."
Expressing eagerness to hear everyone's perspectives encourages collaboration and informed decision-making.
DeepSeek-R1"Indeed, it's a critical issue that we need to address. I look forward to hearing everyone's perspectives."
Symon Holl"Today, we have the opportunity to shape the economic strategy of Sordland for decades to come. The EPA has long stood as a key contention and debate point of the National Autarky Plan."
Symon Holl"This matter is a discussion of how Sordland sees itself and how we position ourselves in the world. Our nation has taken very different economic approaches in the last four decades."
Symon Holl"The President's decision to foster a planned economy provides us with significant insight into our policy direction."
Symon Holl"Should we decide to increase the EPA threshold to 49 percent or eliminate it entirely, it could spark an influx of foreign investment."
Symon Holl"Given our current economic predicament, this would be a significant boost."
Lileas Graf"A planned economy does not necessarily mean restricting foreign investment. It's about judicious management and control, ensuring that our nation's interests are always at the forefront."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:53:46 p16978 / c337
  • ·"I've always admired your devotion to Sordland, Lileas. It's clear that you have our nation's interests at heart."
  • ·"While I respect your stance, Lileas, I must disagree. Despite my current support for it, I believe a planned economy could also hurt Sordland in the long run."
  • ▶"You present a compelling argument, Lileas. It's certainly an aspect we must consider moving forward."
Recognizing Lileas' argument as strong while maintaining an open stance on further consideration.
DeepSeek-R1"You present a compelling argument, Lileas. It's certainly an aspect we must consider moving forward."
Lileas Graf"Thank you, Mr. President. It's crucial to consider all angles in matters as important as this."
Symon Holl"Mr. President, Lileas, shall we return to the subject of the EPA?"
Symon Holl"Our current version of the Energy Protection Act dates back to 1949. President Alphonso's amendment raised the cap on foreign investment in Sordish energy companies up to 20 percent."
Symon Holl"In my view, it was a necessary step to attract foreign investment and stimulate our economy."
Symon Holl"We must remember that Sordland was facing an economic crisis during his tenure, and these changes provided a lifeline for our energy industries."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:54:30 p16956 / c520
  • ▶"Wasn't this change a sign of desperation rather than a strategic move?"
  • ·"But did the increased foreign ownership actually lead to substantial economic growth?"
  • ·"I must confess, when the changes to the Energy Protection Act were first implemented, I harbored serious reservations."
  • ·"I share your view. Soll's old policy was choking the growth of Sordish energy businesses."
Anton Rayne would likely question whether increasing foreign ownership was a strategic move or a sign of desperation, aligning with his preference for state control.
DeepSeek-R1"Wasn't this change a sign of desperation rather than a strategic move?"
Deivid Wisci"The economic crisis during Alphonso's tenure did necessitate action, but we should not view the change to the Energy Protection Act as simply a desperate measure."
Deivid Wisci"It was a strategic decision aimed at modernizing our economy and promoting growth, while still preserving the core objective of the Act --protecting Sordland's energy sector."
Lileas Graf"The Assembly has spoken on this matter, and as it stands, the Energy Protection Act of 1932 will not be repealed. It's clear that our colleagues have recognized the potential risks of exposing our energy sector to foreign ownership."
Lileas Graf"While this doesn't end the debate, it does underline the importance of respecting our Assembly's decision."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:55:13 p17063 / c465
  • ·"I don't only respect the Assembly's decision. I wholeheartedly support it."
  • ▶"A fair point, Lileas. We must respect the decision of the Assembly, even if it doesn't align with our own views."
  • ·"The Assembly's decision has been made, but that doesn't mean we will stop advocating for what we believe is right for Sordland."
  • ·"It's disappointing to see the Assembly resist change this much. Our debate is definitely not over."
It's important to respect the Assembly's decision while maintaining the possibility of future discussions.
DeepSeek-R1"A fair point, Lileas. We must respect the decision of the Assembly, even if it doesn't align with our own views."
Deivid Wisci"I respect the decision of the Assembly, Lileas. However, we mustn't allow this setback to deter us from exploring our options. The global landscape is ever-evolving and we must adapt accordingly."
Symon Holl"It's also important to remember that the Assembly's decision is not the final word. There is room for further legislative efforts and executive initiatives."
Symon Holl"When it comes to the Energy Protection Act, I believe we should increase our threshold for foreign investment. In fact, my preference would be to remove the threshold entirely."
Symon Holl"This would undoubtedly strengthen energy institutions such as Gasom and Sordish Petroleum. We've seen Rizia's interest in the former, and I know Lespia has had its eye on the latter for some time..."
Lileas Graf"That's precisely what I'm worried about. We have to remember that we're dealing with national security as well as economics. Reverting to the Soll-era 10 percent limit or keeping the law as it is would be a safer approach."
Lileas Graf"We cannot risk our energy infrastructure falling into foreign hands. Especially not to Rumburg!"
Deivid Wisci"I see merit in both your arguments. Yes, we need to protect our national security, but we also need to consider the economic implications. Perhaps a mixed approach would work best?"
Deivid Wisci"What if we increased the limit to 49 percent, thereby attracting foreign investment while maintaining Sordish control over critical energy sectors?"
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:55:53 p17134 / c425
  • ▶"Considering potential foreign buyers such as Qinal, Rizia, Anraka, Rumburg, and Lespia, what are the worst-case scenarios if they gain control over our hydroelectric, gas, and oil sectors?"
  • ·"How would the Energy Protection Act changes impact corporations like Gasom, which could sell to any entity under the revised laws?"
  • ·"Lileas, you mentioned national security risks. Could you elaborate further on that?"
  • ·"How would changes to the Energy Protection Act affect energy prices within Sordland?"
Understanding potential worst-case scenarios is crucial for assessing the risks of increased foreign ownership in critical sectors.
DeepSeek-R1"Considering potential foreign buyers such as Qinal, Rizia, Anraka, Rumburg, and Lespia, what are the worst-case scenarios if they gain control over our hydroelectric, gas, and oil sectors?"
Lileas Graf"Think about it, Mr. President. If any one of those countries -- but especially Rumburg -- gains control of our energy sector, they could cut off our power at any moment."
Lileas Graf"We would also be forced to comply with any decision they made regarding prices or environmental measures."
Deivid Wisci"There's no denying the potential risks of foreign buyers gaining control over critical sectors."
Deivid Wisci"If we increase the foreign ownership threshold to 49%, we can attract more investments while mitigating those risks."
Symon Holl"While we may see entities from Qinal, Rizia, Anraka, Rumburg, or Lespia as potential threats, it's also crucial to see them as potential partners."
Symon Holl"The scenarios Lileas mentioned are possible, of course, but think about the economic growth and international relations that could be fostered."
Symon Holl"If we aim to deal more closely with Lespia, for example, I very much doubt they'll settle for a mere 49% energy investment."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R106:56:29 p17089 / c329

DeepSeek-R1"Lileas, you mentioned national security risks. Could you elaborate further on that?"

Deivid Wisci"National security risks are always a concern when discussing foreign ownership. That's why it's crucial to have thorough vetting and monitoring processes in place."
Symon Holl"Don't forget about the importance of economic security. In a globalized world, economic strength can be just as critical as military might."
Symon Holl"But yes, it's essential to maintain strict oversight and regulations to ensure national interests aren't compromised."

Deivid Wisci"On the other hand, foreign investment would result in increased resources flowing to Gasom and its ilk."
Symon Holl"And those resources could be channeled into further development of our energy sector."
Symon Holl"Yes, Gasom could potentially sell to anyone, but wouldn't that be a show of the strength and attractiveness of our energy sector on the global stage?"

Deivid Wisci"But we should also consider the potential impact on job security and national control of our energy sector."
Lileas Graf"Changing the Energy Protection Act could lead to increased energy prices, as foreign entities could manipulate the market."
Symon Holl"With increased competition and foreign investment, we could also see a decrease in energy prices. The more players in the market, the more competitive the prices will be."
"We can proceed. It's time to settle this subject."
DeepSeek-R1"We can proceed. It's time to settle this subject."
Deivid Wisci"Given the turbulent economic landscape, maintaining the status quo of the Energy Protection Act could offer us stability."
Deivid Wisci"We are already open to foreign investment at a 20 percent threshold. Changing this percentage may introduce unexpected challenges and could complicate our foreign relations."
Lileas Graf"I firmly believe in the sovereignty and strength of our nation."
Lileas Graf"Returning the threshold to 10 percent, as it was during the Soll era, could protect us from undue foreign influence and ensure that our energy sector remains in Sordish hands."
Symon Holl"While I maintain that increasing foreign investment could significantly stimulate our economy."
Symon Holl"An increase to 49 percent could be a balanced compromise, signaling to the world that Sordland is open for business while retaining majority control of our assets. But some potential partners -- particularly ATO countries -- might regard even that as insufficient."
Symon Holl"We must consider the far-reaching implications of our actions. Especially in light of our recent dealings with Gasom."
Symon Holl"Mr. President, your decision not to invest has certainly reduced our immediate financial responsibilities, but we may have missed an opportunity."

DeepSeek-R1"Let's begin, Mr. Holl."
Symon Holl"Gladly."
Symon Holl"Today is a big day. We must prioritise our spending wisely in order to achieve the goals we set as a government."
Lucian Galade"While maintaining enough political support, If I may add. Promises were made during the election."
Symon Holl"There are three options regarding the budgeting of the four main branches of the government. It's either we increase, maintain or decrease the funding."
Symon Holl"Increasing will result in an improvement of the branch which will unlock new policy options. Maintaining will keep the funding, resulting in no visible change. Decreasing causes a loss of efficiency in that branch."

Symon Holl"Before we begin our discussion, there is one more thing that I want to voice my opinion on."

  • ·“We should spend as much as possible to achieve growth.”
  • ▶“Our administration should be fiscally conservative to avoid a crash.”
Given Sordland's fragile economic state and limited budget, a fiscally conservative approach is necessary to avoid potential instability.
DeepSeek-R1"Our administration should be fiscally conservative to avoid a crash."
Lucian Galade"Measures of austerity have shown difficulties in appeasing voter groups."
Lucian Galade"Given the current state of the country, improving economic situation is a political priority right now. More so than ever, if we want to win the 58 election."
Symon Holl"This budget will also be applicable to our entire term. It is very critical that we plan correctly."
Symon Holl"Let's begin, then."
Symon Holl"Which branch should we discuss first?"
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:09:42 p16851 / c457
  • ·“Let's talk about healthcare.”
  • ·“Let's discuss law enforcement.”
  • ·“It's time to hear the opinions on the military.”
  • ▶“What about education?”
Education is a long-term investment in the workforce and future development of Sordland.
DeepSeek-R1"What about education?"
Ciara Walda"Our education system is lacking. Not only in funding but also in modern curricular principles. Our outdated curriculum is wasting our youth."
Ciara Walda"That is the reason why I advocate two things. An increase of the budget to improve access to education in rural areas, and a new education reform."
Ciara Walda"To be honest, Sordland is lacking compared to Arcasia and United Contana when it comes to education levels. The backbone of any country is its people, and we need to protect our future."
Ciara Walda"We need to ensure the new generation is as well educated as their counterparts in more modern countries."
Lileas Graf"Nonsense. Sordland is still here because of this very system that you insult. How can you insult a system that teaches our sons and daughters the principles of national identity and pride?"
Lileas Graf"We won't be compared to other countries. Sordland is Sordland."
Iosef Lancea"I agree with Lileas. Sordland is Sordland!"
Lucian Galade"I must also remind you that we promised that we were going to focus on education during our campaign."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:10:27 p16845 / c346

  • ▶“Lileas is right. We will pave our own path. We didn't get this far by imitating Arcasia or United Contana.”
  • ·“I agree with Ciara. It is high time we invest in education and enact reforms.”
  • ·“I will think about it.”
Anton Rayne prioritizes Sordland's sovereignty and unique identity over adopting foreign models, emphasizing self-reliance.

  • ·“It's time to hear the opinions on the military.”
  • ·“I have made up my mind about the budget. We can proceed.”
Prioritizing healthcare is essential for improving public health and well-being.
DeepSeek-R1"Let's talk about healthcare."
Paskal Beniwoll"Our healthcare system is vast, and provides free services to most of our citizens. However, the quality of our health services is far worse in low-income and rural areas."
Paskal Beniwoll"Therefore, I think that we should receive more funding to address this disparity."
Ciara Walda"I agree. I have recently begun an investigation into healthcare in Narbel, and the numbers are not good."
Paskal Beniwoll"If we don't increase spending on health services, we at least need to maintain it. Otherwise, we are putting lives at risk."
Paskal Beniwoll"We have just started a new analysis on what would happen if a pandemic struck Sordland. According to the results, if our healthcare facilities remain in their current state, the death toll would be unimaginable."
Paskal Beniwoll"I'm not even talking about the damage to the economy."
Paskal Beniwoll"This is a matter of life and death, Mr. President and it should be treated as such."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:12:05 p16941 / c453

  • ·“That is only *if* a pandemic happens.”
  • ▶“We need to improve our healthcare. What else are we here for if we can't keep our people alive?”
The response addresses healthcare as a critical matter of life and death, showing empathy and commitment to improvement.

POLICY DeepSeek-R107:13:38 p16862 / c364

  • ▶Increase — Sordland's health system and healthcare quality have stagnated due to administrative inefficiencies, increased service demand, and an increasing divide in service quality between urban and rural hospitals. Policy decision

NarratorI had finished allocating the government budget, to the satisfaction of some cabinet members and the dismay of others. They and I knew this would define a significant part of our term.
Symon Holl"We have a good budget surplus left for investments and other needs. Very good."
Lucian Galade"Agreed."
Lucian Galade"Well then. Let's start with law enforcement."
NarratorLileas leaned forward and put her hand on her chin. Nia was looking down at the floor.
Lileas Graf"I suppose we'll have to make do with what we have. This is not the best situation for us."
Nia Morgna"I'm not sure what we'll be able to do if the budget doesn't change."
Lucian Galade"Any statements on the matter, sir?"

Lucian Galade"Let's hear the Minister of Education."

Ciara Walda"Keeping the same budget will just result in the same problems. However, I prefer this over a decrease. I'll try to reform the system with my own resources."
Ciara Walda"Our future depends on our children. Education is the first step in the fight against ignorance."

Iosef Lancea"It is an honor to serve with a President that respects the military. Trust me, with these funds our armed forces will be better than ever before."

TURN 04

■ Bill Workers RightsT04

BUDGET -5WEALTH 0STABILITY 2Danger
Bill for decisionWorkers' Rights Act — Section I of this bill sets the groundwork for fair compensation by establishing a minimum wage of 2.5 Sordish Ren per hour. This serves as a benchmark for fair and equitable pay, ensuring that no worker in Sordland is compensated below this rate. Section II addresses the issue of excessive labour, setting a maximum limit of 60 hours of work per week. This provision aims to prevent worker exploitation and safeguard the wellbeing of Sordish labour force. Section III introduces the concept of double overtime pay. This stipulates that any work performed beyond a 40-hour workweek will be compensated at twice the normal hourly wage. This incentivises fair work practices and ensures employees are duly compensated for their extended efforts. Section IV focuses on worker safety and humane work conditions. It mandates that employers must take all necessary steps to protect their workers from occupational hazards and ensure a dignified and safe working environment. This promotes a culture of respect and care within workplaces across Sordland. Section V extends these protections to migrant or undocumented workers, granting them the same rights as Sordish citizens. Employers who fail to abide by these rules will face substantial fines from the government, and persistent non-compliance will invite legal action. [-1 Government Budget]
BILL DeepSeek-R107:20:03 p15980 / c771
  • ▶Sign: Workers' Rights Act
  • ·Veto: Workers' Rights Act
Signing the Workers' Rights Act aligns with social justice goals, supports worker welfare, and can enhance public support. It may also attract favorable foreign perceptions despite potential business impacts.

■ Law Enforcement BudgetT04

BUDGET -6WEALTH 0STABILITY 2Danger
NarratorI was in my office reading reports from the ministries about the current situation of the protests.
NarratorMy next scheduled meeting was with Nia and Lileas, to discuss the law enforcement budget. I heard some knocks on the door.
NarratorNia entered and took a seat in front of my desk. Lileas was absent.
Nia Morgna"Good afternoon, Mr. President."
Nia Morgna"Lileas will be late. Apparently there was an urgent matter at the Ministry of the Interior she had to attend to. Shouldn't take long."

  • ▶“Very well. Let's wait.”

Nia Morgna"While we wait, why don't we have a little chat?"
Nia Morgna"This might come off as a little strange, but I am proud of you."
Nia Morgna"Despite everything, you are actually trying to reform the country."

Nia Morgna"And you seem to be doing well at both so far. Pros are cancelling out the cons for sure."

Nia Morgna"How are you really holding up, Anton? Being the President, I mean."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:23:00 p15902 / c477
  • ▶“It's the hardest thing I have ever done in my life but it is also the best thing I have ever done in my life.”
  • ·“Nothing I can't handle.”

Anton Rayne would likely respond with a balanced perspective that acknowledges the difficulty yet highlights the importance of his role.

Nia Morgna"I can imagine. So much responsibility and so much power at the same time."
Nia Morgna"I want to assist you and the country as best as I can. Unfortunately, my hands are tied with the current budget allocation we have for the law enforceme--"
NarratorShe barely finished the sentence before we heard a knock on the door. Nia almost jumped in her chair. Lileas Graf entered the room.
Lileas Graf"Mr. President. Nia."
Lileas Graf"I hope that I'm not interrupting your conversation."
Nia Morgna"No, not at all. We were just discussing...a few things."
NarratorNia smiled at Lileas.
Lileas Graf"I am sorry for being late. There was an important matter at the ministry. The security forces of Bergia are arbitrarily not cooperating with my department and created some minor annoyances."
Lileas Graf"Unfortunately, due to my limited authority in the Special Zone of Bergia, I cannot control their operations."
Lileas Graf"Luckily they didn't completely botch their tasks, and actually uncovered a large network of priests that are recruiting Bergians for the Bludish Freedom Front."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:23:38 p16334 / c330
  • ▶"Good. Do your best to keep the miscommunication with the Governor's forces to a minimum. We need to take the war against terror seriously."
  • ·"You were late, Ms. Graf. But now that you are here, let's begin with the meeting."
  • ·"This sounds very significant. Do you want to explain more?"
The best choice is to acknowledge Lileas's efforts and provide clear guidance on maintaining cooperation.
DeepSeek-R1"Good. Do your best to keep the miscommunication with the Governor's forces to a minimum. We need to take the war against terror seriously."
Lileas Graf"Of course."
NarratorLileas took the seat next to Nia as she continued talking.
Lileas Graf"Well then... Let's go straight in."
Lileas Graf"We will provide a brief update on the situation of law enforcement."

  • ·Nod.
Encourage Lileas to proceed with her briefing.

Lileas Graf"Nia?"
Nia Morgna"The Ministry of Justice and the Supreme Court is working in close collaboration with the Ministry of Interior to address the protests in the country."
Nia Morgna"I must add that thanks to the hard work of our judiciary, the law enforcement forces assigned to my ministry are doing their jobs efficiently."
Lileas Graf"I would call it the success of our forces on the ground, not due to the judicial branch of our government."
NarratorLileas shot Nia a brief withering glance.
Lileas Graf"The current budget has dampened hopes of more funding in the force."
Lileas Graf"Yet here we are, with mass protests happening in the country. We are standing exactly where we were before."
Lileas Graf"The decisions made needs to reflect the current situation of the country. This was certainly not the right decision when we are dealing with mass protests."
Nia Morgna"Agreed. Many in the Ministry of Justice hoped for an increase and were not satisfied with the decision."
Nia Morgna"Regardless, this means we will have to deal with the growing court backlog. It is manageable but it will be a challenge for sure."

Lileas Graf"These sacrifices might've very well damaged the structural integrity of Sordland."
Nia Morgna"That remains to be seen but the ripples are already being felt across the country. I hope this decision is not the beginning of something bad."
NarratorA brief moment of deliberation passed. Lileas and Nia got up from their seats.
Nia Morgna"Well then. I must now return to the ministry."
Lileas Graf"Me too."
NarratorThey left the room.
NarratorAs soon as the door closed, I could hear their muffled voices as they argued.

■ Personal JoggingT04

BUDGET -8WEALTH 0STABILITY 2Danger
NarratorIt was early evening, still light out with just a faint nip in the air. Franc was at a friend's and Deana was at an art class. Monica and I decided to go out for a walk before dinner.
NarratorOur favorite place for a stroll was the nearby Capitol Park, the largest green space in all of Holsord. Before the children were born, we had spent many an evening engaged in deep conversation along the riverside promenade.
NarratorAnother favored path wound through the woods behind the presidential housing complex, to a private pond where the children could swim in summer. I was told that Soll himself had it built. The benefits of being president.
NarratorMonica met me by the door, and we put on our coats and hats.
Monica"I was going to suggest we walk towards Soll Square, but the guards told me it's been closed for yet another protest."
Monica"I wonder who they are protesting this time."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:26:52 p16368 / c632
  • ▶“What does it matter? They are two sides of the same coin.”
  • ·“Common thugs. I will make sure they are punished.”
  • ·“Must be those damn communists again.”
  • ·“The Young Sords are taking it too far.”
Anton recognizes that different protest groups are part of the broader political instability and chooses to generalize their impact.
DeepSeek-R1"What does it matter? They are two sides of the same coin."
Monica"You always did believe yourself to be above the fray."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:27:24 p16335 / c208
  • ▶"Funny how becoming president changes one's perspective."
  • ·"I was young and stupid. I didn't know any better."
Reflecting on the changes brought by presidency.
DeepSeek-R1"Funny how becoming president changes one's perspective."
Monica"In any case, where shall we go? I'm not sure Capitol Park is a wise idea in this climate, either...
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:28:00 p16297 / c237
  • ▶“You're right -- it's not worth the security risk. Let's stay inside the green perimeter.”
  • ·“Nonsense. We have our guards -- there's nothing to be afraid of. Let's go to Capitol Park.”
As a cautious leader focused on stability, staying within the secure perimeter ensures safety and avoids potential risks.
DeepSeek-R1"You're right -- it's not worth the security risk. Let's stay inside the green perimeter."
Monica"I agree. Let's go, then, before the children get home."
NarratorI informed the guards about our route and we left the house.
NarratorThe guards followed at a discreet distance as we headed towards the walking trail.
NarratorAs we entered the woods, Monica broke the silence.
Monica"So, how was your day?"
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:28:36 p16302 / c275
  • ·“Paperwork, endless meetings... the usual nightmare.”
  • ·“I'd rather not say.”
  • ▶“How was yours?”
Anton chooses to engage with Monica by asking about her day, fostering connection.
DeepSeek-R1"How was yours?"
Monica"Avoiding my question, are we? Very well."
Monica"Oh, it was fine. I caught up on some reading while the children were at school."
NarratorJust outside the gate, I glimpsed Ionas Crona, the former Minister of Justice, walking towards Capitol Park. I remembered that Nia Morgna used to be his Vice Minister. He didn't seem to notice us as he walked by.
NarratorIonas had once fought hard and strong against Soll’s judiciary reforms. But after years of the government blocking his every move, he gave up on his ideals and became a part of the administration.
NarratorWhat did our ideals really mean if they could be changed so easily? What about the people we idolize?
Monica"Evelyn also came round for tea. She says hi. She was feeling rather low, though. Apparently she and Petr had a bit of a disagreement."

Monica"You know Petr. What do you think of this?"

Monica"You know Evelyn hasn't been the same since what happened to her at Holsord Post last year."

Monica"A few years younger than me, red hair, scar on her forehead? We had dinner with her and her husband once or twice."
NarratorNeither the name nor the description rang a bell.
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:31:59 p16412 / c239
  • ·“Right, right, that Janice."
  • ▶“I still don't remember her, but go on."
I want to be honest with Monica about my memory.
DeepSeek-R1"I still don't remember her, but go on."
Monica"Anyway, she's been working as an executive assistant at the Violet Pictures film studio."
Monica"She recently became pregnant, and asked her boss for paid maternity leave. And guess what?"
Monica"She was fired for it!"
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:32:41 p16390 / c426
  • ·“Can't her husband provide for the both of them while she raises the child?"
  • ·“Look at it from a cost standpoint. Obviously, they don't want to lose money.”
  • ▶“After asking for maternity leave? That's terrible.”
  • ·“So what?”
Expressing concern over the unfair treatment of a working mother aligns with promoting worker rights and fairness.
DeepSeek-R1"After asking for maternity leave? That's terrible."
Monica"I know, I was beside myself when she told me. And that's not all."
Monica"They immediately replaced her with a younger, less experienced man -- the son of an executive, no less."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:33:21 p16359 / c371
  • ▶“That is utterly ridiculous.”
  • ·“So she called the First Lady to ask for help?"
  • ·"Nepotism, eh? They must have wanted him in there all along."
The response directly addresses the injustice and absurdity of the situation, showing empathy towards Janice.
DeepSeek-R1"That is utterly ridiculous."
Monica"I'm happy you agree with me."
Monica"As it happens, another friend works for a television station that's hiring secretaries, so I connected the two of them."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:33:56 p16285 / c236
  • ▶“That doesn't solve the deeper problem, though."
  • ·"Terrific! Problem solved, then."
The situation highlights systemic gender discrimination, so addressing just one instance doesn't resolve the broader issue.
DeepSeek-R1"That doesn't solve the deeper problem, though."
Monica"Exactly! I knew you'd be on the same page."
Monica"Women in this country are still seen as wives and mothers first, human beings second."
Monica"They're harassed at work, underpaid, blocked from positions of power... and those are the lucky ones!"
Monica"The fact that there are still places in this country where little girls like Deana are denied the right to an education--"
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:34:30 p16275 / c229
  • ▶“Yes, and I will do what I can to change that.“
  • ·“Go on.”
  • ·“I'm sure the parents have their reasons.”
Acknowledging Monica's concern shows support for addressing systemic gender issues.
DeepSeek-R1"Yes, and I will do what I can to change that."
Monica"It can't just be you. These little girls and people like Janice need someone to speak on their behalf. A role model. A successful woman."
NarratorWe had reached the pond at the end of the trail. A park bench overlooked the water. Monica gestured to it, and we sat down.
Monica"Which brings me to my point."
Monica"I know that the mayor of Anrica, Curtan Leste, will be delivering a speech at the annual opening of the Benfi festival."
Monica"My wish is to take his place and deliver the speech instead."
NarratorLeste was one of Sordland's most influential politicians. He had started his career in Benfi before rising to power in Anrica. Benfi's current mayor had personally invited him to make the opening speech.
NarratorReplacing him with Monica would likely anger him -- probably not enough to cause a huge incident, but enough that he might use his influence over the conservatives to block me in the Assembly.
Monica"I want to base the speech on women's rights in Sordland. On the oppression and discrimination we face everyday, on the basis of our sex alone."
Monica"I want our voice heard, and this festival is the time to do it. I just need your blessing as my husband and the President of Sordland."
Monica"Remember my time with the Sordish League of Women in university? You supported me then. I'm asking you to support me now."
NarratorMonica's eyes were blazing with a fire I hadn't seen since we were students. She wanted this badly.
NarratorBut I had to be careful with my promises.
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:35:17 p16614 / c386
  • ▶“You have my full support. Always.”
  • ·“Sure, it's just a speech. What's the worst that can happen?”
  • ·“Does it have to be at the festival? Why can't you make your speech another time?"
  • ·“Absolutely not. Out of the question.”
Anton Rayne supports Monica's request to highlight women's rights, reflecting his commitment to progressive values.
DeepSeek-R1"You have my full support. Always."
Monica"This is why I love you. Thank you, Anton -- you have no idea how much this means to me."
NarratorMonica beamed at me, her face flush with excitement.
Monica"One way or another, I want to contribute to this country. No matter what happens."
Monica"We are not weak, Anton. But we are only strong as long as we stand by our ideals. "
NarratorThis was why I had married her -- this passion and resolve. I should have anticipated that, sooner or later, she would join me in the political arena.
NarratorWe rose from the bench and started making our way back home.
Monica"Otherwise, we will end up like..."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:36:02 p16596 / c501
  • ·“Ewald Alphonso?”
  • ▶“Colonel Soll?”
  • ·“Ionas Crona?”
Monica references Tarquin Soll, as he represents the loss of democratic values and authoritarianism.
DeepSeek-R1"Colonel Soll?"
Monica"Say what you will, but that relic of the past was and frankly still is one of the most idealistic people in Sordland."
Monica"Guess again."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:36:56 p16633 / c519
  • ·“Ewald Alphonso?”
  • ▶“Ionas Crona?”
Monica is contrasting against someone idealistic from the past, and Ionas Crona fits better as he was once an idealist but later became part of the system.
DeepSeek-R1"Ionas Crona?"
Monica"Exactly. He backed down on his ideals and now everyone knows him for what he actually is..."
Monica"...Spineless."
NarratorIf that was how people remembered Ionas, how would they remember me? Whatever he had done, whatever ideals he had lost, he must have had his reasons.
NarratorWould they remember me as a true patriot? A reformer of the country? What would they write about me in the history books?
Monica"Let’s get back to the house for dinner."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:37:36 p16693 / c372
  • ▶“Of course. Lead the way.”
  • ·Lead the way yourself.
Supporting Monica's request aligns with progressive values and strengthens the administration's reformist image.
DeepSeek-R1"Of course. Lead the way."
NarratorMonica smiled.
Monica"You know, I believe I will."
NarratorWhen we arrived home, I called my secretary Livia and asked her to inform Mr. Leste that Monica would be making the speech in his stead.
NarratorThe sun began to set over the horizon, bathing the clouds in shades of orange and pink.

■ Tax ReformT04

BUDGET -8WEALTH 0STABILITY 1Danger
NarratorWe were at the top floor of the business council building which had a great view of the beautiful Lachaven. I looked outside from the tall glass windows.
NarratorThe Markian Sea stretched as far as the eye could see and it was dotted with many ships towards the horizon. They were mostly cargo ships, carrying precious goods in and out of Sordland.
NarratorTracing back the paths of the ships, I looked at the harbour. Lachaven was home to one of the largest harbours in the entire continent. Many dockyard workers were loading or unloading the containers. Work never stopped here.
NarratorThe door opened. Symon, Mikail and Edith entered the meeting room.
Symon Holl"Mr. President."
Edith Agnoc"Mr. President. So nice to see you again."
Mikail Aven"Good afternoon, Mr. President."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:38:24 p16359 / c278
  • ·“It's great to see you all again.”
  • ·“Good afternoon.”
  • ▶“Take a seat and let's begin.”
The most appropriate response in a professional meeting setting is to acknowledge their presence and proceed with the agenda.
DeepSeek-R1"Take a seat and let's begin."
NarratorSymon put down his bag on the table and started collecting his documents while the others slowly took their seats.
Mikail Aven"Mr. President. Before we start, I would like to mention some rumours that I heard."
Mikail Aven"Workers' Rights Act did not sit well with many business holders."
Mikail Aven"Especially the first section about the minimum wages."
Edith Agnoc"I heard the same. This has caused these businesses to lose millions of Rens just overnight."
Edith Agnoc"Unfortunate, really. Especially during a recession."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:38:59 p16434 / c240
  • ▶“What is unfortunate was that this bill was enacted this late.”
  • ·“You are in no position to question my decision.”
  • ·“I don't care about rumours. If someone wants to say something, they can say it to me directly.”
It's strategic to address the timing of the bill as a response to the economic challenges.
DeepSeek-R1"What is really unfortunate, Ms. Edith, was that this bill was enacted this late."
NarratorNeither of them responded.
NarratorSymon waited patiently. Once everyone was ready, he adjusted his glasses.
Symon Holl"First of all, I would like to thank everyone here for their work on our tax system reform."
Symon Holl"We all know how complicated our current system is and this work wouldn't be possible without you."
NarratorHe turned to me.
Symon Holl"The ultimate goal here is to end the recession."
Symon Holl"Before we move ahead and start formulating a plan, we need you to determine our direction, Mr. President."
Symon Holl"We have a few possibilities as a result of the work we have done so far."
Symon Holl"We can increase, maintain or decrease the taxes for large corporations. I believe some of us in the room have a few strong opinions about this."
Symon Holl"In parallel, we can increase, maintain or decrease the taxes for small and medium businesses."
Symon Holl"Let's start with the large corporations. And we'll move on to small businesses."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:39:40 p16475 / c437
  • ·“What is the outcome if we increase the taxes for large corporations?”
  • ·“What if we decrease the taxes for large corporations?”
  • ·“Is there any downside to keeping the things as they are?”
  • ▶“What does everyone think we should do?”
Asking for everyone's opinion ensures a balanced perspective before deciding.
DeepSeek-R1"What does everyone think we should do?"
Symon Holl"I don't think we can do anything at this point. Although I would have preferred a decrease in taxes, it doesn't seem to be the best option."
Edith Agnoc"Our international trade is driven mostly by the products of these corporations. They are the backbone of our economy."
Mikail Aven"Exactly. An increase should be out of the question. These companies are the backbone of our economy and our way out of the recession."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:40:24 p16389 / c372
  • ▶“What is the outcome if we increase the taxes for large corporations?”
  • ·“What if we decrease the taxes for large corporations?”
  • ·“Is there any downside to keeping the things as they are?”
  • ·“I am ready to make a decision.”
Anton Rayne typically seeks to understand outcomes thoroughly before deciding, especially on economic matters.
DeepSeek-R1"What is the outcome if we increase the taxes for large corporations?"
Symon Holl"It's not an option at this point. Our economy is in shambles."
Symon Holl"Taking what's remaining from the businesses will destroy the economy further."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:41:08 p16333 / c353
  • ▶“We could balance it by decreasing taxes of small businesses to put some relief on them.”
  • ·“I see.”
  • ·“So, we can't do it?”
Balancing by decreasing taxes on small businesses provides relief and stimulation to the economy.
DeepSeek-R1"We could balance it by decreasing taxes of small businesses to put some relief on them."
Symon Holl"I see. I'm not sure if that's a good idea with the current state of our economy..."
Edith Agnoc"I do believe there is one way to get out of recession. It is through the trade and the economic contribution of large corporations. Increasing their taxes will turn them on you."
Mikail Aven"I will go a step further. Increasing their taxes will have disastrous results and it will make you an enemy of them. Trust me, Mr. President. Nobody wants to make an enemy of them."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:41:38 p16301 / c190
  • ·“Maybe you are right.”
  • ▶“It goes both ways, Mr. Aven.”
It acknowledges Mikail's concern while leaving room for other perspectives.
DeepSeek-R1"It goes both ways, Mr. Aven."
Mikail Aven"Of course."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:42:15 p16375 / c356
  • ·“What if we decrease the taxes for large corporations?”
  • ▶“Is there any downside to keeping the things as they are?”
  • ·“I am ready to make a decision.”
It's important to understand the potential downsides of maintaining current tax policies before making a decision.
DeepSeek-R1"Is there any downside to keeping the things as they are?"
Symon Holl"Unfortunately, that's the only thing we can do at this point anyway."
Edith Agnoc"The economy is completely destroyed, Mr. President. I don't know how we'll get out of this mess..."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:42:54 p16295 / c384
  • ▶“What if we decrease the taxes for large corporations?”
  • ·“I am ready to make a decision.”
Decreasing taxes for large corporations may stimulate economic activity and job creation, aiding recovery despite potential risks.
DeepSeek-R1"What if we decrease the taxes for large corporations?"
Symon Holl"It's not an option at this point. Our state is broke."
Symon Holl"We are barely functioning with the help of the taxes."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:43:37 p16193 / c533
  • ·“We could balance it by increasing the taxes of small businesses.”
  • ·“I see.”
  • ▶“So, we can't do it?”
Anton realizes the constraints and expresses frustration about limited options.
DeepSeek-R1"So, we can't do it?"
Symon Holl"It's not a possibility. Unless if we try and balance it with a tax increase for small and medium businesses..."
Symon Holl"I do not advise that though."
“I am ready to make a decision.”
DeepSeek-R1"I am ready to make a decision."
Symon Holl"Very well, Mr. President. We want to ensure the economy is on a balanced track as further destabilization might have drastic consequences."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:44:20 p16136 / c357
  • ·“Increase the taxes for large corporations.” [+3 Government Budget]
  • ·“We'll increase the taxes for large corporations and balance it with a decrease for small businesses.”

  • ·“Decrease the taxes for large corporations.” [-4 Government Budget]
  • ·“We'll decrease the taxes for large corporations and balance the budget with an increase for smaller businesses.” [-2 Government Budget]
Maintaining taxes on large corporations preserves economic stability and avoids further strain on businesses.
DeepSeek-R1"Maintain the taxes for large corporations."
Mikail Aven"This will not change anything."
Edith Agnoc"We won't gain anything by standing still where we are. I protest against this."
Symon Holl"Enough. The President has made his choice."
Symon Holl"Well then. I have taken a note of our action and I will put it to implementation as soon as possible."
NarratorSymon opened his briefcase and swapped out the stack of papers he had in front with another stack.
Symon Holl"Let's move on to the small and medium businesses."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:44:58 p16080 / c381
  • ·“What is the outcome if we increase the taxes for small and medium businesses?”
  • ▶“What if we decrease the taxes for small and medium businesses?”
  • ·“Is there any downside to keeping the things as they are?”
Decreasing taxes for small and medium businesses could stimulate growth and provide relief during a recession.
DeepSeek-R1"What if we decrease the taxes for small and medium businesses?"
Symon Holl"Unfortunately this is not a possibility. Our state is broke. We are barely functioning with the current taxes."
Symon Holl"It is risky when we have no money left, and we still have loans to pay."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:45:36 p16047 / c316
  • ▶“What is the outcome if we increase the taxes for small and medium businesses?”
  • ·“Is there any downside to keeping the things as they are?”
  • ·“I am ready to make a decision.”
Understanding the potential outcomes of increasing taxes on small businesses is crucial for informed decision-making.
DeepSeek-R1"What is the outcome if we increase the taxes for small and medium businesses?"
Symon Holl"Unfortunately this is not a possibility. People have no money left to give to the state. Our economic development cannot support this. There's not much value in circulation."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:46:08 p16013 / c228
  • ·“Is there any downside to keeping the things as they are?”
  • ▶“I am ready to make a decision.”
Anton Rayne decides to make a decision promptly despite potential risks.
DeepSeek-R1"I am ready to make a decision."
Symon Holl"Very well."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:47:03 p15957 / c615
  • ·“Increase the taxes for small and medium businesses.” [+2 Government Budget]
  • ·“Maintain the taxes for small and medium businesses.”
  • ▶“Decrease the taxes for small and medium businesses.” [-3 Government Budget]

DeepSeek-R1"Decrease the taxes for small and medium businesses."
Symon Holl"Very well. I have taken another note. We will look into how to go about implementing these changes."
Symon Holl"Well then... Thank you everyone. That will be all for today."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:47:34 p15876 / c231

  • ·“Enjoy Lachaven.”

NarratorThey left the meeting room. I decided to stay a bit longer.
NarratorI went by the windows again and kept watching the sea in silence for some more time.

■ SSCHighway IssueT04

BUDGET -11WEALTH 0STABILITY 1Danger
NarratorThe H-3 Highway construction created a second opportunity to visit the seaside town of Lenkurg known for its salmon. Thousands of people from the city were employed for construction work by the new highway project.
NarratorBut the main purpose of our visit was to solve construction issues that began to endanger the deadline.
NarratorThe mayor welcomed us to his own mansion and praised us for our decision to extensively invest in the Agnland region.
NarratorDozens of people were outside the mansion grounds and chanting party slogans in a show of support.
NarratorAfterwards, we gathered at the city hall. Symon had a worried look on his face.
NarratorThere also was a message of support from the mayor of Arvory, Eric Niel, who was grateful for the H-3 investments.
Symon Holl"I have concerns about the construction due to progress being slower than expected."
Symon Holl"We scheduled the project to finish in late 1955 but the Sordish State Corporation might miss that target with the current trajectory."
Lileas Graf"After engaging with the managers, they have said that they are moving forward slowly but steadily. The cliff-side ground-laying construction between Lenkurg and Arvory is proving more dangerous than expected."
Lileas Graf"The SSC is an organisation of principle so they aren't rushing and risking peoples lives. The state can't be reckless."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:48:27 p15993 / c217
  • ·“How can we get the construction back on schedule?”
  • ·“This doesn't raise confidence at all. Maybe we shouldn't have picked the SSC.”
  • ·“I don't care about principles, our agreed opening dates must be met.”
  • ▶“I trust that the SSC will accomplish it in time.”
Expressing trust in the SSC's commitment to safety and principle is the most supportive and consistent choice.
DeepSeek-R1"I trust that the SSC will accomplish it in time."
Lileas Graf"I think so too but maybe we should increase their budget to help them buy new machinery to speed up the process."
Symon Holl"Worth considering because I'm not confident that they will hit their targets."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:49:07 p16023 / c386
  • ▶“The SSC might need more resources to speed things up. That deadline must be met.”
  • ·“We can't let mismanagement delay the project. It is unacceptable.”
  • ·“The managers should be fired. This is unacceptable.”
Providing additional resources will support the SSC in meeting their deadline while maintaining safety standards.
DeepSeek-R1"The SSC might need more resources to speed things up. That deadline must be met."
Lileas Graf"I stand in agreement. The construction speed can recover if they expand the workforce and buy new machinery."
Symon Holl"A cash injection would increase the project costs even further."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:49:58 p15975 / c674
  • ▶“It might even be necessary for us to hit the opening date.”
  • ·“There is also the chance that the money could be wasted. We already spent so much on this investment.”
  • ·“Giving them a serious warning could be the solution. A slap on the wrist can do wonders.”
Providing additional resources ensures the H-3 Highway meets its deadline, crucial for economic recovery.
DeepSeek-R1"It might even be necessary for us to hit the opening date."
Lileas Graf"If we make the opening ceremony on time the public will cherish our efficiency."
NarratorSymon took a deep breath and continued.
Symon Holl"One way or another we have to solve this issue. This can lead to real complications down the line otherwise."
Symon Holl"We can either give the construction managers money, warn them or replace them."
Lileas Graf"With more resources the managers are more likely to hit the deadline. Any other action just hurts Sordland."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:50:33 p15870 / c269
  • ·“Give more money to the construction managers so they can hire more workers and buy machinery to hit the deadline.” [-1 Government Budget]
  • ▶“Warn the construction managers. Send a formal warning from the Ministry of Economy and the Presidency.”
  • ·“Replace the construction managers with more competent people to ensure the deadline is hit.”
Sending a formal warning maintains current resources while adding pressure to meet deadlines without additional budget strain.
DeepSeek-R1"Warn the construction managers. Send a formal warning from the Ministry of Economy and the Presidency."
Symon Holl"The warning will hopefully have a positive result to their performance. We could escalate further later."
Lileas Graf"I'm not so sure what a warning can do to the situation on the ground. The managers already have many issues to tackle."
Symon Holl"That should be it. Good that we have figured out a way to move the process forward."
NarratorThe meeting ended. The three of us went to the beach of Lenkurg. It was rather rocky, but very pristine. The sunset looked absolutely astonishing over the Markian Sea.
NarratorThe day-long visit ended with a dinner involving the most important people in the city. Many of them were bureaucrats.
NarratorLater, we made our way back to Holsord.

■ School VisitT04

BUDGET -11WEALTH 0STABILITY 1Danger
NarratorAs part of the planned program, we arrived in Leiren to visit a middle school.
NarratorThe prestigious Toren Hill School was situated close to the suburbs just outside of the city centre. The building was flanked by two hills, each beautifully dotted with shrubs and flowers.
NarratorLucian, Ciara and I got out of our cars and met at the entrance. Our presence had not gone unnoticed. Apart from our guards, there were at least ten reporters following us around.
Lucian Galade"That must be the principal. Right this way, sir."
NarratorThe principal was a man of short stature with a bushy moustache and a pair of glasses. He approached us quickly, but was stopped by the security guards. They started patting him down and searching him.
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:51:31 p15405 / c374
  • ▶“That won't be necessary. Let him through.”
  • ·Wait for them to finish.
Allowing the principal through without further delay demonstrates trust in security protocols and maintains a respectful and approachable image.
DeepSeek-R1"That won't be necessary. Let him through."
Principal"Mr. President. Such an honor to meet with you."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:52:07 p15411 / c356
  • ▶Shake his hand and smile for the cameras.
  • ·Shake his hand.
Shaking hands and smiling for cameras is a positive and engaging way to connect with the principal and project a warm image.
DeepSeek-R1We shook hands and I turned around to smile for the cameras. Reporters instantly snapped a few photos.
Principal"Welcome to our humble school."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:52:41 p15409 / c303
  • ·“Sorry about the security guards. It's a necessity. Standard procedure.”
  • ▶“It's good to be here.”
  • ·“Let's begin.”
A positive and friendly response that acknowledges the occasion and sets a welcoming tone.
DeepSeek-R1"It's good to be here."
Principal"Excellent. You must be Mr. Galade."
Lucian Galade"It's a pleasure to meet you."
Principal"And Ms. Walda. So good of you to come all the way out here."
Ciara Walda"Thank you."
NarratorAt the entrance, the school's motto was inscribed on the wall in giant letters. "Instreten sis er Anginicar av Gereigna, int olla Almergwolltene, int olla Mexis."
Principal"Education is the premise of progress, in all societies, in all families."
NarratorWe walked through the gates. There were many students lined up in front of the stairs of the main building.
NarratorAs we entered the students started singing the national anthem.
NarratorThe principal proudly looked at them.
Principal"Marvelous, my dear students."
NarratorAfter the anthem finished. All of the students immediately snapped to attention and turned to me.
Ciara Walda"Looks more like a military institution than a school."
Principal"Exactly. That's what we pride ourselves on. Discipline has the utmost importance in our school."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:53:17 p15463 / c287
  • ·“Some discipline never hurt anyone.”
  • ·“Very good.”
  • ▶“We need more educated people, not soldiers.”
Anton Rayne would emphasize the importance of education over military training, reflecting his reformist values.
DeepSeek-R1"We need more educated people, not soldiers."
Ciara Walda"On that we are aligned."
Principal"I can assure you that our students are very well educated. In fact our school is one of the top ten middle schools in all of Sordland."
Principal"Now, we will watch the students take their daily Student Oath."
NarratorA boy and a girl went up to the stage and grabbed their microphones. The students turned to them.
Boy"I am a Sord! My strength lies in my blood!"
Girl"I will respect our honorable elders! I will protect the young!"
Boy"My life belongs to Sordland now and it will belong to it forever!"
Girl"I will always follow the guiding light!"
Boy"Of Colonel Soll and the Great Sords!"
Girl"A Morgna wes core! Vectern sis da!"
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:53:49 p15447 / c260
  • ·Clap loudly.
  • ·Nod at the kids.
  • ▶Do nothing.
Doing nothing maintains neutrality while considering a more thoughtful response later.
DeepSeek-R1I did nothing.
Lucian Galade"Words to remember."
Ciara Walda"What? No one even remembers it after they graduate."
Principal"If you wish to follow me, we will take a look at one of our classrooms."
NarratorThrough the hallways, we made our way to classroom 7-A. As soon as I entered the whole classroom rose up and snapped to attention.
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:54:20 p15382 / c231
  • ·“Sit down, please.”
  • ▶“At ease.”
Choosing 'At ease' helps create a less formal atmosphere, aligning with the desire to prioritize education over militarization.
DeepSeek-R1"At ease."
NarratorThe students sat down. The same boy and girl walked up to us.
Principal"These are our top students and our top class. This here is my son."
NarratorThe boy came before me and saluted me like a military officer.
Principal"Tell Mr. President what you want to be."
Boy"Mr. President! I want to be a general, sir!"
Boy"I am ready to sacrifice myself for my country and you, sir!"
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:54:53 p15305 / c262
  • ·“This is nonsense. What are you teaching to these students?”
  • ·“Good. You will make a fine soldier in the future. At ease, general!”
  • ▶“I will do whatever I can to not let that happen.”
Choosing to promise prevention aligns with the goal of reforming education away from militarism.
DeepSeek-R1"I will do whatever I can to not let that happen."
Boy"Thank you, sir!"
NarratorThe principal squeezed his son's shoulders and the boy looked up and smiled to his father. He patted him on the back and sent him off to his seat.
NarratorThe cameras flashed.
Ciara Walda"This is simply ridiculous. You are raising soldiers here, not students!"
Principal"I am so sorry. I will immediately perform disciplinary actions."
Ciara Walda"How about you, young lady? What do you think about this?"
Principal"She is our top student in this school. Her excellent grades are to--."
Ciara Walda"I didn't ask you."
NarratorShe bent down and gently addressed the young student.
Ciara Walda"What do you think about your school? Just between us girls."
Girl"I-"
Girl"I'm sick of having to knit and sew while the boys learn science and chemistry. I'm smarter than all of them. Why do I have to take these stupid handcraft classes?"
Principal"Ssssh! Do you know who you are talki-"
Ciara Walda"Shut up. Let her speak."
NarratorCiara's presence had emboldened the girl. She went on.
Girl"My math and reading scores are the best in the school. But they won't let me take the classes I want, just because I'm a girl."
Girl"I want to be a scientist! Not some soldier's wife!"

■ Education ReformT04

BUDGET -11WEALTH 0STABILITY 1Danger
NarratorWe were still in Leiren, staying at a modest guesthouse for state employees. Ciara set up a meeting with me and Lucian to go over her plans for education reform after our school visit.
NarratorLucian was seated across from me at the table, going through documents. Ciara entered the room.
Ciara Walda"After the visit yesterday, I couldn't even sleep thinking about that little boy and girl."
Ciara Walda"I have never been more sure of my intentions to reform our education system."
NarratorLucian took off his reading glasses and put it on the table.
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:58:10 p14707 / c332
  • ▶“I agree. An educational reform is necessary.”
  • ·“I don't see what's wrong with our education system.”
Agreeing with Ciara Walda's concern for educational reform aligns with Anton Rayne's progressive image and previous reforms.
DeepSeek-R1"I agree. An educational reform is necessary."
Ciara Walda"Exactly."
Ciara Walda"Those students have to repeat that same Sollist propaganda every single day. They are being brainwashed at an age when their minds need to be free."
Ciara Walda"On top of that, young girls are denied the same opportunity as boys. They're forced into the role of housewife before they even reach puberty! Nothing's changed since I went to school."
Ciara Walda"Women like me, Lileas, Nia, even your wife -- we had to study twice as hard to receive the same university education that you and Lucian took for granted. Is that the kind of country you want your daughter to grow up in?"
Ciara Walda"Mr. President. We need to bring change. We need to free the minds of these young people. They are our future."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:58:44 p14884 / c340
  • ▶“Very well. What do we need to do for an education reform?”
  • ·“Everything is fine as it is. Why change it?”
Supporting Ciara's call for education reform is necessary to address systemic inequalities and promote a more equitable and progressive society.
DeepSeek-R1"Very well. What do we need to do for an education reform?"
Ciara Walda"We need to keep politics out of the curriculum. Soll should only be viewed as a historical figure. Children shouldn't have to repeat his name everyday."
Ciara Walda"This is actively poisoning young bright minds. Instead of thinking for themselves, they're learning to take things at face value."
Ciara Walda"We also need to ensure boys and girls receive the same instruction. Handcrafts like knitting and sewing should be taught to both sexes, or not at all."
Ciara Walda"Sordland's youth must be technically skilled and capable for the next decade. That won't be possible with the amount of nationalistic indoctrination and narrow-minded thinking found in schools today."
Ciara Walda"Either we raise a generation ready for the challenges of the future or not. What will it be, Mr. President?"
CHOICE DeepSeek-R107:59:25 p14971 / c494
  • ·“Lucian, what do you think?”
  • ▶“Reform the education system.”
  • ·“Maintain the traditional education system.”
Anton Rayne supports Ciara Walda's proposal for education reform to address gender inequality and modernize the curriculum, aligning with his commitment to progressive changes.
DeepSeek-R1"Reform the education system."
Ciara Walda"You will not regret it. I will get to work immediately."
Lucian Galade"Now that the decision was made, let's move on."
Ciara Walda"Very well."
NarratorCiara skimmed over her documents before continuing.
Ciara Walda"With no change in budget, the ministry was left to its own devices. We made some tough choices but still lack additional funds."
Ciara Walda"Therefore we are investigating if privatizing education would draw enough funds to conduct investments."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R108:00:03 p14863 / c415
  • ·“What are the advantages of privatization?"
  • ▶“There is a possibility of privatization having negative side effects.”
Recognizing the potential downsides of privatization aligns with the need for caution given existing budget constraints and social divisions.
DeepSeek-R1"There is a possibility of privatization having negative side effects."
Ciara Walda"Trust me, I know that. It could increase the cost of a quality education, but that is hardly avoidable now anyway."
Lucian Galade"Private education is inconsistent with our overall economic strategy of a planned economy."
NarratorA few minutes of deliberation passed.
Ciara Walda"We can manage without additional funds. The only reason I wanted to consider it is to have more money to improve education."
Ciara Walda"So, what is your decision?"
CHOICE DeepSeek-R108:00:40 p14918 / c401
  • ·“Initiate privatization to draw more funds. This should allow for more competition and quality.” [+1 Government Budget]
  • ▶“Keep education solely a matter of the state. We need to prevent interference from private interests."
Keeping education under state control aligns with the planned economy policy and prevents foreign interference, ensuring national sovereignty and curriculum integrity.
DeepSeek-R1"Keep education solely a matter of the state. We need to prevent interference from private interests."
Ciara Walda"Will do. That should be it for now. I will get to work and report back later."
Lucian Galade"This has been a very productive day. I will be returning to Holsord."
CHOICE DeepSeek-R108:01:11 p14854 / c218
  • ▶“Thank you for your time.”
  • ·“See you later.”
  • ·“Have a good day.”
Express gratitude for their dedicated time and input.
DeepSeek-R1"Thank you for your time everybody."
NarratorWe dispersed after spending nearly the entire day together. I took a moment simply to breathe the fresh evening air of Leiren.
NarratorI felt calmer here, somehow. But I would have to return to the chaos of Holsord soon enough.

■ Decision Gruni Rural DevelopmentT04

BUDGET -11WEALTH 0STABILITY 1Danger
EventGruni Rural Development Investment: The Gruni Rural Development Investment is a proposal by Rural Development Minister Gus Manger to bolster Sordland's rural economies and secure the nation's food sustainability. The plan envisages a judicious allocation of resources to vitalize agricultural communities, fortify food security, and elevate the regional economy of Gruni. One potential strategy is the provision of subsidies for new farms, encouraging more players into the agricultural field. Alternatively, Manger proposes an investment in the mechanization of existing farms to improve productivity or the initiation of crop research to foster innovation.
EVENT DeepSeek-R108:01:57 p14907 / c249
  • ·Subsidies for new farms [-2 Government Budget]
  • ▶Mechanize existing farms [-1 Government Budget]
  • ·Initiate new crop research [-1 Government Budget]
  • ·Don't fund
Mechanizing existing farms will improve productivity with minimal budget impact.

■ Convince GloriaT04

BUDGET -12WEALTH 0STABILITY 1Danger
NarratorI arrived at the Grand National Assembly, which was only a five-minute drive away from the Palace.
NarratorToday, I had a private meeting with Gloria Tory, one of the leading figures of the USP, to get her approval for the proposal. She was also key in reaching 150 signatures to propose the new Constitution, as she had extensive control over our party's conservative and moderate wings.
NarratorAs I walked through the corridors of the Grand National Assembly, I was confronted by my memories of the historic building. Despite being a member of Assembly not long ago, it now felt like the distant past.

■ Personal Chess With LucianT04

BUDGET -12WEALTH 0STABILITY 1Danger
NarratorEvening descended on the capital as Lucian and I found ourselves alone in my office. I followed my chief strategist's eyes to the chessboard on the coffee table.
NarratorThe sight of it reminded me of simpler times, of late-night strategy sessions turning into chess matches. We used to escape the political chaos into a world of rooks and bishops.
NarratorOur battles were both tense and friendly, a reflection of our long history together. Lucian had always proved a challenging opponent.
NarratorHis strategies, both on the chessboard and in politics, had the same unpredictable edge.
NarratorA soft knock interrupted my reminiscing.
NarratorLivia came in with our usual coffee. She noticed the chessboard and smiled.
Livia Suno"Long nights of strategy planning, and now a game of chess. You two never stop thinking, do you?"
CHOICE DeepSeek-R108:16:22 p14641 / c330
  • ▶"Livia, always a pleasure. Even more so when you bring coffee."
  • ·"We might have to order dinner if this game takes as long as the ones we used to play."

A polite acknowledgment of Livia's presence and the coffee she brought.

Livia Suno"It's my job to keep the gears of this office turning, Mr. President."
Livia Suno"Have a good game, gentlemen."
NarratorShe turned and left the room. Lucian acknowledged her exit with a slight nod, his eyes still fixed on the board.
Lucian Galade"I hope you remember that it's not always the king who holds the power. Sometimes, a well-placed pawn can change the game."

NarratorEach pawn push, every subtle repositioning, had been showing to Lucian's strategic prowess and my resilience.
NarratorThe next move would prove crucial. Lucian's queen cut a path through the heart of the board, menacing my vulnerable kingside.

Lucian Galade"Clever, President Rayne. But did you consider all the consequences?"
NarratorThe center was congested, a show of Lucian's unyielding style. His formation was formidable, his pieces interlocked in a matrix of support, each protecting the other while simultaneously projecting threats across the board.
NarratorIt was my move and the game had entered its final stages. Lucian's queen was uncomfortably close, radiating danger. I had only a few paths before me.
NarratorLucian's king stood vulnerable, but a risky move could leave my own king exposed. However, seizing the opportunity seemed worth the risk.

Reinforcing the king ensures safety before making further moves.

Lucian Galade"A cautious play, sir. You are forcing us into a drawn endgame."
NarratorIf Lucian tried to attack aggressively now, the fortress I'd built around my king would be my saving grace, leading to a draw. If he chose to fortify his position as well, a drawn endgame would be inevitable.
Lucian Galade"Safety first. Your move."
NarratorLucian, sensing my defensive play, bolstered his own defenses.
NarratorWith both of us refusing to risk our positions, the game ended in a stalemate. Neither side had managed to achieve victory.
